content:
# John Chapter 14 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 14 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 14:1 — "Let not your hearts be troubled" vs. "Don't let your hearts be troubled" and "Believe" vs. "Trust"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Let not the heart of you be troubled; believe in the God, and in me believe." | Literal; articles preserved; inverted imperative |
| LOGOS | "Let not your hearts be troubled. Believe in God; believe also in me." | Formal equivalent; "also" for kai |
| PNEUMA | "Don't let your hearts be troubled. Trust in God, and trust also in me." | "Trust" for pisteuete; "Don't" for me |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Believe" is the standard rendering of pisteuO in John and connects to the Gospel's central theme (20:31). "Trust" (PNEUMA) is a valid synonym but loses the Johannine vocabulary consistency.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The formal "Let not" preserves the gravitas of Jesus' opening words in the Farewell Discourse.

---

## 14:2 — "many rooms" vs. "many dwelling places" vs. "more than enough room"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "In the house of the Father of me are many dwelling places" | Literal monai; "dwelling places" preserves the noun form |
| LOGOS | "In my Father's house are many rooms" | "Rooms" — simplest English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "There is more than enough room in my Father's home" | "Home" for oikia; "more than enough room" — interpretive expansion; shifts from plural noun to collective |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "rooms." The Greek monai is plural and countable — "many rooms" preserves this. PNEUMA's "more than enough room" loses the plural specificity and adds an evaluative judgment ("more than enough").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "dwelling places" is technically precise but LOGOS's "rooms" is clearer. The connection to monE in v.23 is noted in commentary.

---

## 14:2-3 — "If it were not so, would I have told you that I go to prepare a place?" vs. restructured clause

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and if not, I would have told you, for I go to prepare a place for you" | "For" — causal reading of hoti |
| LOGOS | "If it were not so, would I have told you that I go to prepare a place for you?" | Rhetorical question; "that" for hoti |
| PNEUMA | "If this were not so, would I have told you that I am going to prepare a place for you?" | Similar to LOGOS; "this" for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The rhetorical question reading is more natural in English and captures the reassuring tone.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's causal reading ("for") is a legitimate alternative but less natural.

---

## 14:3 — "I will come again and will take you to myself" vs. "I will come and get you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I come again and will take you to myself" | Present tense "I come" (erchomai) for futural present |
| LOGOS | "I will come again and will take you to myself" | Future tense in English; "to myself" for pros emauton |
| PNEUMA | "When everything is ready, I will come and get you, so that you will always be with me where I am" | "Everything is ready" expands the conditional; "get you" is colloquial; "always" adds permanence |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Take you to myself" (paralempsomai hymas pros emauton) is a tender, deliberate phrase — "get you" (PNEUMA) is too casual for this promise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's additions ("everything is ready," "always") expand beyond the Greek.

---

## 14:5 — Thomas's question: formal vs. colloquial

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lord, we do not know where you go. How are we able to know the way?" | Literal dynameth; historical present |
| LOGOS | "Lord, we do not know where you are going. How can we know the way?" | Natural English |
| PNEUMA | "No, we don't know, Lord... We have no idea where you are going, so how can we know the way?" | Adds "No"; "have no idea" amplifies ignorance; conversational tone |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Thomas's question is earnest, not emphatic — "we have no idea" and "No, we don't know" add emotional intensity not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:7 — "If you had known me, you would have known my Father also" vs. "If you have known me, you will know"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If you have known me, you will know the Father of me also" | Reads egnOkate as indicative + future (real condition) |
| LOGOS | "If you had known me, you would have known my Father also" | Reads as contrary-to-fact condition (second class) |
| PNEUMA | "If you had really known me, you would know who my Father is" | Adds "really"; mixed conditional |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The textual tradition is divided — Sinaiticus supports the indicative reading, but the context (Jesus is gently rebuking the disciples' lack of understanding) favors the contrary-to-fact condition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's reading follows Sinaiticus more closely but the contrary-to-fact reading makes better contextual sense.

---

## 14:8 — "it is enough for us" vs. "it suffices us" vs. "we will be satisfied"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it suffices us" | Literal arkei hemin |
| LOGOS | "it is enough for us" | Natural English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "we will be satisfied" | "Satisfied" shifts from sufficiency to satisfaction |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "it is enough for us." Clear, faithful, and natural.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "satisfied" introduces a psychological dimension not present in arkei.

---

## 14:9 — "Whoever has seen me" vs. "Anyone who has seen me" and "How can you say" vs. "So why are you asking"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] having seen me has seen the Father. How do you say, 'Show us the Father'?" | Literal participle; "How do you say" |
| LOGOS | "Whoever has seen me has seen the Father. How can you say, 'Show us the Father'?" | "Whoever" — universalizes; "How can you" |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father! So why are you asking me to show him to you?" | Exclamation; "why are you asking" rephrases the question |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "How can you say" preserves the Greek pOs su legeis — the question is about the statement, not the act of asking.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's rephrasing changes the rhetorical structure.

---

## 14:10 — "on my own authority" vs. "from myself" vs. "not my own"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I do not speak from myself" | Literal ap' emautou |
| LOGOS | "I do not speak on my own authority" | Adds "authority" to clarify the idiom |
| PNEUMA | "The words I speak are not my own" | Restructures entirely — attributive rather than agentive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "On my own authority" captures the sense of ap' emautou in context — Jesus is not freelancing; he speaks as the Father's authorized representative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's bare "from myself" is ambiguous. PNEUMA's restructuring loses the agentive construction.

---

## 14:15 — "you will keep" vs. "keep" vs. "obey"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "keep the commandments of me" | Imperative reading of terEsete (some MSS) |
| LOGOS | "you will keep my commandments" | Future indicative — a promise/expectation, not a command |
| PNEUMA | "obey my commandments" | "Obey" for tereO; imperative reading |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's future indicative "you will keep." This reads the verb as a natural consequence of love rather than a separate command — love leads to keeping.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The textual variant (imperative vs. future indicative) is noted. PNEUMA's "obey" has a harsher tone than "keep" (tereO).

---

## 14:16 — "Advocate" vs. "Helper" vs. "Advocate"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "another Advocate" | Parakletos rendered as Advocate |
| LOGOS | "another Helper" | Parakletos rendered as Helper |
| PNEUMA | "another Advocate" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Advocate" (EMET/PNEUMA). The legal/forensic sense of parakletos (one called alongside to assist, particularly in a judicial setting) is better captured by "Advocate" than "Helper." The Spirit's role in John includes testifying (15:26) and convicting (16:8) — forensic functions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/PNEUMA. This is a notable case where the SOPHIA consensus departs from LOGOS. "Helper" is too generic for the Johannine Paraclete.

---

## 14:16 — "to be with you forever" vs. "to the age" vs. "who will never leave you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that he may be with you to the age" | Literal eis ton aiOna |
| LOGOS | "to be with you forever" | Standard English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "who will never leave you" | Negative formulation — "never leave" instead of "be with forever" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "forever." PNEUMA's negative formulation is a valid dynamic rendering but changes the emphasis from presence to non-abandonment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:17 — "the Spirit of truth" vs. "the Holy Spirit, who leads into all truth" and pronoun: "him" vs. "it" vs. "he"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Spirit of the truth, which the world is not able to receive... it abides and in you it will be" | Neuter pronoun "it" following Greek pneuma (neuter) |
| LOGOS | "the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot receive... he dwells with you and will be in you" | Masculine pronoun "he/him" — personal Spirit |
| PNEUMA | "He is the Holy Spirit, who leads into all truth" | Identifies Spirit of truth as Holy Spirit explicitly; "leads into all truth" is from 16:13, not this verse |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's masculine pronoun rendering. While the Greek uses neuter pronouns (agreeing with pneuma), English convention and the personal activities attributed to the Spirit (teaching, reminding) justify personal pronouns.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's neuter pronouns are grammatically accurate to the Greek but misleading in English. PNEUMA imports content from 16:13 into this verse.

---

## 14:20 — "In that day" vs. "When I am raised to life again"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "In that day" | Literal en ekeinE tE hEmera |
| LOGOS | "In that day" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "When I am raised to life again" | Interprets "that day" as the resurrection specifically |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "In that day" (EMET/LOGOS). The referent of "that day" is deliberately open — it could mean the resurrection, Pentecost, or the parousia. PNEUMA's specification narrows what John leaves open.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 14:22 — Judas's question: "how is it that" vs. "why are you going to" and identification

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Ioudas — not the Iskariotes — says to him, 'Lord, what has happened that to us you are about to manifest yourself and not to the world?'" | Literal ti gegonen hoti |
| LOGOS | "Judas (not Iscariot) said to him, 'Lord, how is it that you will manifest yourself to us, and not to the world?'" | "How is it that" — smooth English question |
| PNEUMA | "Judas (not Judas Iscariot, but the other disciple with that name) said to him, 'Lord, why are you going to reveal yourself only to us and not to the world at large?'" | Extensive parenthetical; "reveal" for emphanizO; adds "only" and "at large" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The parenthetical identification is sufficient as "(not Iscariot)." PNEUMA over-explains.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:23 — "make our home with him" vs. "dwelling with him we will make"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to him we will come and dwelling with him we will make" | Literal; monEn par' autO poiEsometha |
| LOGOS | "we will come to him and make our home with him" | "Home" for monEn — the same root as v.2 |
| PNEUMA | "we will come and make our home with each of them" | "Each of them" — pluralizes the individual promise |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Make our home" beautifully connects to the "rooms" (monai) of v.2 — the same root word (monE) now describes God dwelling in the believer.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "each of them" adds a distributive element not in the singular Greek pronoun.

---

## 14:26 — "Advocate" vs. "Helper" and "bring to your remembrance" vs. "will remind you of everything"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, which the Father will send in the name of me, that [one] will teach you all things and will remind you of all things that I said to you" | "Advocate"; neuter "which" |
| LOGOS | "the Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things and bring to your remembrance all that I have said to you" | "Helper"; "bring to remembrance" |
| PNEUMA | "when the Father sends the Advocate as my representative — that is, the Holy Spirit — he will teach you everything and will remind you of everything I have told you" | "As my representative" for en tO onomati mou; adds interpretive gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted SOPHIA's own rendering with "Advocate" (per v.16 decision) and "bring to your remembrance" (LOGOS phrasing). PNEUMA's "as my representative" is an interpretive expansion of "in my name."

**Status:** Resolved primarily in favor of LOGOS's phrasing with "Advocate" substituted for "Helper."

---

## 14:27 — "Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you" vs. "I am leaving you with a gift — peace of mind and heart"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Peace I leave to you, the peace of me I give to you" | Literal; preserves emphatic word order and repetition |
| LOGOS | "Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you" | Preserves structure; semicolon separates the two gifts |
| PNEUMA | "I am leaving you with a gift — peace of mind and heart" | "Peace of mind and heart" interprets eirEnE; collapses the two clauses |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The double statement ("Peace I leave... my peace I give") is rhetorically powerful and must not be collapsed. PNEUMA's "peace of mind and heart" psychologizes what is a comprehensive Hebrew shalom.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The parallel structure is essential to the verse's rhythm and meaning.

---

## 14:30 — "He has no claim on me" vs. "in me he has nothing" vs. "He has no power over me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in me he has nothing" | Literal en emoi ouk echei ouden |
| LOGOS | "He has no claim on me" | Interprets "has nothing in me" as a legal/ownership metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "He has no power over me" | "Power over" — interprets as authority/dominion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "He has no claim on me." The phrase suggests the ruler of this world has no foothold, no legal right, no point of leverage in Jesus. "Claim" captures this forensic/ownership sense.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's literal rendering is ambiguous. PNEUMA's "power over" narrows to domination, missing the legal nuance.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adding interpretive content not in Greek | 7 | "more than enough" (v.2), "everything is ready" (v.3), "really" (v.7), "as my representative" (v.26), "peace of mind and heart" (v.27), "only/at large" (v.22), "raised to life again" (v.20) |
| PNEUMA substituting dynamic equivalents | 4 | "Trust" for pisteuO (v.1), "obey" for tereO (v.15), "get you" for paralambano (v.3), "power over" for echei ouden (v.30) |
| Key theological term divergence | 3 | parakletos: Advocate/Helper (vv.16, 26), monE: rooms/dwelling places/room (v.2), eirEnE structure (v.27) |
| EMET preserving Greek grammatical features | 3 | Neuter pronouns for Spirit (v.17), historical presents, article preservation |
| LOGOS departing from SOPHIA consensus | 1 | "Helper" vs. consensus "Advocate" (v.16) — rare LOGOS departure |
| Conditional/mood differences | 2 | v.7 (indicative vs. contrary-to-fact), v.15 (future vs. imperative) |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

John 14 presents the most theologically dense material yet, and the translator divergences correspondingly touch on major doctrinal issues. The most significant disagreement is over parakletos (v.16, 26) — a rare case where SOPHIA's consensus sides with EMET/PNEUMA ("Advocate") over LOGOS ("Helper"), recognizing the forensic-legal dimension of the Spirit's role in John.

PNEUMA's tendency to add interpretive content is especially visible in this chapter, where the temptation to explain and expand Jesus' promises is strong. The most problematic additions are "peace of mind and heart" (v.27), which psychologizes shalom, and "When I am raised to life again" (v.20), which specifies what John deliberately leaves open with "In that day."

EMET's neuter pronoun for the Spirit (v.17) raises a genuine translation question: should English follow Greek grammatical gender or theological conviction about the Spirit's personhood? The consensus follows LOGOS in using personal pronouns, but EMET's note about the Greek grammar is textually important.

The chapter shows LOGOS and SOPHIA in their closest alignment yet — nearly verse-for-verse identical except for the parakletos decision.

---
*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — 2026-03-05*
